explicit_text: I was born in Nagano Prefecture on June 15, 1888. When I returned to Japan in 1913, my home was still in Nagano-ken, and my big brother was the head of the family. It was there that I registered my marriage in 1914, following the traditional sake-drinking ceremony. Nagano was my family’s home base while I was building my life abroad.
implicit_text: I was born in a mountainous region in central Japan on June 15, 1888. When I returned to Japan in 1913, my home was still in that prefecture, and my big brother was the head of the family. It was there that I registered my marriage in 1914, following the traditional sake-drinking ceremony. That area was my family’s home base while I was building my life abroad.

explicit_text: I was just a child during the Sino-Japanese War, so I don’t remember much except that Japan won. It was a distant event for me, but I knew it was important for the country. That early victory was part of Japan’s growing militaristic spirit that I would witness more closely later.
implicit_text: I was just a child during the conflict between Japan and China, so I don’t remember much except that Japan won. It was a distant event for me, but I knew it was important for the country. That early victory was part of Japan’s growing militaristic spirit that I would witness more closely later.

explicit_text: During the Russo-Japanese War, I was a high school student and remember well how Japan became a militaristic nation. There was a lot of confusion, but we were winning, which stirred strong feelings of nationalism. It was a formative time for me, seeing my country assert itself on the world stage.
implicit_text: During the war between Japan and Russia, I was a high school student and remember well how Japan became a militaristic nation. There was a lot of confusion, but we were winning, which stirred strong feelings of nationalism. It was a formative time for me, seeing my country assert itself on the world stage.

explicit_text: By the time World War I started, I was already in the United States running a business called Nippon Shokuryo Shin, which sold Japanese food. I was making money during that period despite the war overseas. It was a time when I was focused on building my livelihood in America rather than the conflict itself.
implicit_text: By the time the global conflict that began in 1914 started, I was already in the United States running a business called Nippon Shokuryo Shin, which sold Japanese food. I was making money during that period despite the war overseas. It was a time when I was focused on building my livelihood in America rather than the conflict itself.

explicit_text: The Manchurian Incident happened during the 1930s, right when the Great Depression was hitting. As a Japanese person living in America, life was tough because Nippon-jin were not popular then. It was a difficult time for our community, facing economic hardship and social challenges.
implicit_text: The incident in northeastern China happened during the 1930s, right when the Great Depression was hitting. As a Japanese person living in America, life was tough because people of my heritage were not popular then. It was a difficult time for our community, facing economic hardship and social challenges.

explicit_text: When the Kantō earthquake struck around 1923, I was in America working as a grocery man and president of the Japanese Grocers Association. We sent two tons of flour to Japan through relief agencies, and the shipping companies transported it for free. It was a moment when our community in the US reached out to help those back home.
implicit_text: When the massive earthquake struck the Kanto region around 1923, I was in America working as a grocery man and president of the Japanese Grocers Association. We sent two tons of flour to Japan through relief agencies, and the shipping companies transported it for free. It was a moment when our community in the US reached out to help those back home.

explicit_text: I remember hearing a missionary from Canada when I was about 15 or 16 years old. He was from the Episcopal Church and was invited by our school principal to speak. I listened but didn’t really understand religion at that time; I didn’t have the 'ears' for it then.
implicit_text: I remember hearing a missionary from a North American country when I was about 15 or 16 years old. He was from the Episcopal Church and was invited by our school principal to speak. I listened but didn’t really understand religion at that time; I didn’t have the 'ears' for it then.

explicit_text: The missionary who came to speak at my school was from the Episcopal Church in Canada. Although I didn’t grasp much of his message, it was my first exposure to Christianity. This happened when I was a teenager in Japan, around 1903 or 1904.
implicit_text: The missionary who came to speak at my school was from the Episcopal Church in a neighboring country to the north. Although I didn’t grasp much of his message, it was my first exposure to Christianity. This happened when I was a teenager in Japan, around 1903 or 1904.

explicit_text: I arrived in Seattle straight from Yokohama on the Tosa Maru ship when I was 19 years old. Seattle was known for having a more sincere immigration office compared to San Francisco, which is why many Japanese immigrants landed there. From Seattle, some went on to places like Riverside, California, but I started my life in the Pacific Northwest.
implicit_text: I arrived in a major Pacific Northwest city straight from Yokohama on the Tosa Maru ship when I was 19 years old. That city was known for having a more sincere immigration office compared to San Francisco, which is why many Japanese immigrants landed there. From there, some went on to places like Riverside, California, but I started my life in the Pacific Northwest.

explicit_text: I traveled to America aboard the Tosa Maru, a small 5000-ton ship operated by Nippon Yusen Kaisha (NYK) Line. The ship was cramped, like a transport for animals rather than people, with shelves on the walls where we slept. The journey took 18 days from Yokohama to Seattle, and it was my first experience at sea.
implicit_text: I traveled to America aboard the Tosa Maru, a small 5000-ton ship operated by a major Japanese shipping line. The ship was cramped, like a transport for animals rather than people, with shelves on the walls where we slept. The journey took 18 days from Yokohama to Seattle, and it was my first experience at sea.

explicit_text: I departed from Yokohama on the Tosa Maru when I came to America at age 19. It was the port where many Japanese immigrants began their journey overseas. The ship left directly from Yokohama to Seattle without stopping in Hawaii.
implicit_text: I departed from the main port city near Tokyo on the Tosa Maru when I came to America at age 19. It was the port where many Japanese immigrants began their journey overseas. The ship left directly from that port to Seattle without stopping in Hawaii.

explicit_text: The Tosa Maru was the ship I took from Yokohama to Seattle in 1907. It was a small vessel, about 5000 tons, and conditions were tough—there were shelves on the walls where we slept, and it felt more like animal transport than a passenger ship. The trip lasted 18 days, and many of us were seasick at first but got used to it by the end.
implicit_text: The Tosa Maru was the ship I took from the main Japanese port near Tokyo to Seattle in 1907. It was a small vessel, about 5000 tons, and conditions were tough—there were shelves on the walls where we slept, and it felt more like animal transport than a passenger ship. The trip lasted 18 days, and many of us were seasick at first but got used to it by the end.

explicit_text: When I first came to America, I heard stories about a gold rush in Alaska and thought it would be easy to make money there. Of course, we didn’t realize the gold wasn’t in a spendable form, so that dream didn’t pan out. Still, Alaska represented hope and opportunity for many of us newcomers.
implicit_text: When I first came to America, I heard stories about a gold rush in the far northwestern territory and thought it would be easy to make money there. Of course, we didn’t realize the gold wasn’t in a spendable form, so that dream didn’t pan out. Still, that region represented hope and opportunity for many of us newcomers.

explicit_text: When I arrived in the US, it was winter and cold, and people were preparing for the 1905 presidential election. The economy was depressed, and many Japanese immigrants struggled to find work. This election period was part of the backdrop to my early days in America.
implicit_text: When I arrived in the US, it was winter and cold, and people were preparing for the 1905 presidential election. The economy was depressed, and many Japanese immigrants struggled to find work. This election period was part of the backdrop to my early days in America.

explicit_text: Many Japanese immigrants, including some of my friends, worked for the Great Northern Railway when jobs were scarce. The railroad companies recruited us to work in labor camps, often with groups of 20 to 30 men. Although I didn’t work for the railroads myself, I knew many who did and saved money that way.
implicit_text: Many Japanese immigrants, including some of my friends, worked for a major northern transcontinental railroad when jobs were scarce. The railroad companies recruited us to work in labor camps, often with groups of 20 to 30 men. Although I didn’t work for the railroads myself, I knew many who did and saved money that way.

explicit_text: The Northern Pacific Railway was one of the companies that recruited Japanese laborers during the early 1900s. At times, there were up to 2,000 Japanese working for these railroads. This was a common starting point for many young immigrants trying to earn money in America.
implicit_text: One of the big railroad companies in the northern United States was recruiting Japanese laborers during the early 1900s. At times, there were up to 2,000 Japanese working for these railroads. This was a common starting point for many young immigrants trying to earn money in America.

explicit_text: Along with the Great Northern and Northern Pacific, the Oregon–Washington Railroad and Navigation Company hired many Japanese workers for labor camps. These jobs didn’t require experience, so many young immigrants took them to save money. It was part of the early immigrant labor experience in the Pacific Northwest.
implicit_text: Along with the other two major northern railroads, a regional railroad and navigation company hired many Japanese workers for labor camps. These jobs didn’t require experience, so many young immigrants took them to save money. It was part of the early immigrant labor experience in the Pacific Northwest.

explicit_text: I heard there was a difference between the San Francisco and Seattle immigration offices, with Seattle being more sincere. Because of this, many Japanese immigrants preferred to land in Seattle even if their final destination was California. San Francisco was a major port but had a tougher reputation among immigrants.
implicit_text: I heard there was a difference between the immigration offices in San Francisco and Seattle, with Seattle being more sincere. explicit_text: Many Japanese immigrants who landed in Seattle were headed to Riverside, California, to work picking oranges. It was common for newcomers to move from Seattle down to Riverside for agricultural work. Some of our church members also made that journey, showing how widespread this migration was.
implicit_text: Many Japanese immigrants who landed in Seattle were headed to a city in southern California to work picking oranges. explicit_text: I lived on Vashon Island when the Japanese community started raising strawberries for the first time around 1908. We lived in tents without flush toilets, and there were about 50 strawberry patches. It was a tough but hopeful time, and a Christian minister from Japan even came to speak to us then.
implicit_text: I lived on an island near Seattle when the Japanese community started raising strawberries for the first time around 1908. We lived in tents without flush toilets, and there were about 50 strawberry patches. It was a tough but hopeful time, and a Christian minister from Japan even came to speak to us then.

explicit_text: As Vashon Island became crowded with strawberry farms, many Japanese farmers moved to Bainbridge Island to continue growing strawberries. We shared tools and helped each other in those early days. Bainbridge became another important center for Japanese agricultural work in the region.
implicit_text: As one island became crowded with strawberry farms, many Japanese farmers moved to a neighboring island to continue growing strawberries. explicit_text: I served as president of the Japanese Grocers Association while living in America. During the Kantō earthquake relief efforts in 1923, our association sent two tons of flour to Japan. I also helped organize and support the Japanese grocery community, though I didn’t need help myself at the time.

I also helped organize and support the Japanese grocery community, though I didn’t need help myself at the time.

explicit_text: I first heard about Christianity from a missionary who visited my school in Japan when I was 15 or 16. Later, a Christian minister from Japan spoke to us on Vashon Island in 1908. Although I didn’t understand much at first, these experiences introduced me to the religion during my youth and early immigrant years.
